author: Sidhant Thole
name: Investigating the Tied-Embedding Bottleneck: Why Boundary Blocks Underperform and What It Means for 16MB Models
blurb: Weight analysis of tied-embedding models reveals a symmetric V-shaped capacity profile where boundary blocks (first and last) contribute 2-4x less than middle blocks. Embedding decoupling (embed_dim < model_dim) activates the last block by +42-51% and flattens capacity distribution from 3.7x to 2.1x, but parameter loss from smaller embeddings outweighs the architectural gain. Documents the bottleneck mechanism, the rate-distortion constraints of the 16MB budget, and identifies residual low-rank projection as the promising follow-up direction.
over_budget_note: 16.44 MB artifact exceeds 16 MB limit due to fp16 passthrough of embed_proj and head_proj. Submitted as experimental/analysis record, not as competitive entry.
technique_summary: Embedding decoupling (embed_dim=416, model_dim=512) + extended parallel residuals (start=7) + tuned GPTQ clip_sigmas (12.0) + TTT block freezing (blocks 0-1)
key_findings: Tied embeddings create symmetric V-shaped capacity profile: boundary blocks contribute 2-4x less than middle
Decoupling activates last block by +42-51% and flattens capacity from 3.7x to 2.1x min/max ratio
Parameter loss from smaller embedding outweighs architectural improvement on BPB
Lower clip_sigmas increases compressed artifact size despite reducing quantization error
x0 pathway unused by 9/11 blocks, skip gates suppress boundary contamination, block 10 parallel routing half-dead — all symptoms of tied-embedding constraint
Residual low-rank projection (I+AB, rank-32, 65K params) identified as promising alternative that avoids parameter loss
